coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and physical characterization of carbon quantum dots from watermelon seed towards a biological application
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Puchaicela, Marlene
Lara, David
Cevallos, Vinicio J.
Garzón, Alexis
Gardener, Jules
Solorzano, Guillermo
Spencer, Lilian
Chimborazo, Johnny
coredata.dc:description:
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) incorporated into hydrogels are promising materials for drug delivery applications, especially wound dressings. Its green synthesis, using biomass such as watermelon seeds, offers important economic, environmental, and technological advantages. This aligns with the principles of the circular economy and supports their potential for biomedical use. In this work, the CQDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal method from watermelon seeds. Their physicochemical properties were thoroughly characterized using multiple techniques, including high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), fluorescence microscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Hydrogels were prepared using polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC) to assess CQDs’ antimicrobial activity and cytotoxicity, both in their pure form and integrated into the hydrogels. HRTEM analysis revealed that CQDs exhibited a quasi-spherical morphology with an average diameter of approximately 12–13 nm, as well as, AFM measurements confirmed a similar size of diameter distribution with an average height of 0.385 nm. Raman spectroscopy identified two dominant peaks at
1340
cm
− 1
and
1590
cm
− 1
, corresponding to the disordered D-band and the crystalline G-band, respectively. FTIR spectroscopy indicated the presence of functional groups, including hydroxyl, amine,
s
p
2
and
s
p
3
hybridized CH bonds, carbonyl, alkene/alkyne, amide, ether, and CO bonds. XPS analysis confirmed the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements, while fluorescence microscopy revealed strong, sustained photoluminescence in the blue range. Lastly, biological tests showed that CQDs, in isolation, did not exhibit significant antimicrobial activity. However, cytotoxicity assessments demonstrated that CQDs in pure form were non-toxic at a concentration of 0.03 mg/ml. Conversely, when integrated into PVA and HPMC hydrogels, a toxic effect was observed at the same concentration. When combined with HPMC alone, a slight toxicity was observed. These findings suggest that CQDs’ high photoluminescence and minimal cytotoxicity make them excellent candidates for hydrogel-based drug delivery systems in wound care applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Hydrothermal synthesis
Physical characterization
Hydrogels
Antimicrobial activity
Cytotoxicity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots via hydrothermal method
Hydrogel based on polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) fully hydrolyzed
Antimicrobial sensitivity test: agar disk diffusion method
Antimicrobial assays of CQDS, CQDs-PVA, and CQDs-HPMC

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: After the completion of the hydrothermal treatment, three distinct phases are obtained: a thin layer or surface suspension, a liquid phase containing dispersed CQDs, and residues settled at the bottom of the reactor flask.
Standardized methodology for the synthesis of CQDs from watermelon seeds.
Methodology used for the preparation of hydrogels. PVA: Polyvinyl Alcohol, HPMC: Hydroxypropyl Methylcellulose.
UV–Vis spectrum of liquid phase after hydrothermal synthesis.

(a) HRTEM image of CQDs with a quasi-spherical morphology, showing lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, confirming their graphitic structure. (b) AFM image of CQDs dispersed on a substrate, with different measurement points identified. (c) Height profile of an individual CQD, highlighting its nanometric thickness. (d) Height profile graph corresponding to the section highlighted in (c), shows variations in the topography of the CQDs.

(a) SEM image showing CQD aggregates (141 nm) with a quasi-spherical morphology. (b) AFM image provides a three-dimensional high-magnification view of aggregated CQDs. (c) DLS size distribution confirms polydispersity and aggregation. (d) Zeta potential analysis indicating surface charge and stability.
Left: CQDs solution under daylight. Right: CQD under UV light emits this wavelength at 365 nm.
Fluorescence of level I CQDs using Leica CMS GmbH confocal microscope.

Top: In the left image, the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) hydrogel without CQDs does not fluoresce. In the right image, the PVA hydrogel with embedded CQDs shows fluorescence. Bottom: In the left image, the HPMC gel without CQDs does not fluoresce. In the right image, the HPMC gel with embedded CQDs shows fluorescence. CQDs: Carbon Quantum Dots, HPMC: Hydroxypropyl Methylcellulose.

Cytotoxicity test results for compounds at 0.03 mg/ml concentrations after 4 h. The horizontal axis represents the experimental compounds, while the percentage of cell viability is presented in the vertical axis. Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), CQDs-PVA: Carbon Quantum Dots embedded into Polyvinyl Alcohol, CQDs-HPMC: Carbon Quantum Dots embedded into Hydroxypropyl Methylcellulose.
Comparison of CQD synthesis via hydrothermal methods using different precursors.

Antimicrobial activity of CQDs, CQDs-PVA, and CQDs-HPMC at different concentrations. The data shown are the mean of two replicates, and instances where no inhibition was observed are indicated with a dash (-). CQDs: Carbon Quantum Dots, CQDs-PVA: Carbon Quantum Dots embedded into Polyvinyl Alcohol, CQDs-HPMC: Carbon Quantum Dots embedded into Hydroxypropyl Methylcellulose.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and physical characterization of carbon quantum dots from watermelon seed towards a biological application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) incorporated into hydrogels are promising materials for drug delivery applications, especially wound dressings. Its green synthesis, using biomass such as watermelon seeds, offers important economic, environmental, and technological advantages. This aligns with the principles of the circular economy and supports their potential for biomedical use. In this work, the CQDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal method from watermelon seeds. Their physicochemical properties were thoroughly characterized using multiple techniques, including high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), fluorescence microscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Hydrogels were prepared using polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC) to assess CQDs’ antimicrobial activity and cytotoxicity, both in their pure form and integrated into the hydrogels. HRTEM analysis revealed that CQDs exhibited a quasi-spherical morphology with an average diameter of approximately 12–13 nm, as well as, AFM measurements confirmed a similar size of diameter distribution with an average height of 0.385 nm. Raman spectroscopy identified two dominant peaks at
1340
cm
− 1
and
1590
cm
− 1
, corresponding to the disordered D-band and the crystalline G-band, respectively. FTIR spectroscopy indicated the presence of functional groups, including hydroxyl, amine,
s
p
2
and
s
p
3
hybridized CH bonds, carbonyl, alkene/alkyne, amide, ether, and CO bonds. XPS analysis confirmed the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements, while fluorescence microscopy revealed strong, sustained photoluminescence in the blue range. Lastly, biological tests showed that CQDs, in isolation, did not exhibit significant antimicrobial activity. However, cytotoxicity assessments demonstrated that CQDs in pure form were non-toxic at a concentration of 0.03 mg/ml. Conversely, when integrated into PVA and HPMC hydrogels, a toxic effect was observed at the same concentration. When combined with HPMC alone, a slight toxicity was observed. These findings suggest that CQDs’ high photoluminescence and minimal cytotoxicity make them excellent candidates for hydrogel-based drug delivery systems in wound care applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials have revolutionized various fields, including electronics, energy storage, and biomedicine, offering innovative solutions for next-generation technologies [1]. Among these, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered significant attention due to their tunable fluorescence, high photostability, and biocompatibility, making them ideal candidates for diverse applications [2]. CQDs, typically less than 10 nm in size, are particularly promising for bioimaging, drug delivery, and biosensing, thanks to their excellent optical properties and low toxicity [3].

Hydrothermal synthesis has emerged as a scalable and environmentally friendly method to produce CQDs from carbon-rich precursors [4]. In this study, watermelon seeds, a sustainable and nutrient-rich source, are used as a green precursor. This approach not only reduces waste but also enhances CQD yield, with bioactive compounds such as alkaloids and flavonoids contributing to improved functionality of synthesized dots [5]. This method supports the principles of green chemistry by using renewable resources, advancing the development of eco-friendly nanomaterials [6].

CQDs hold significant promise for biomedical applications, particularly in drug delivery and bioimaging [7]. In drug delivery systems (DDSs), CQDs can be functionalized to selectively target diseased cells, such as cancer cells, improving the delivery and efficacy of therapeutic agents [8,9]. Their tunable surface chemistry allows for precise modifications to enhance drug loading and controlled release, while their small size facilitates deep tissue penetration. Moreover, CQDs outperform conventional dyes and semiconductor quantum dots in bioimaging applications, offering superior photostability, multicolor imaging capabilities, and real-time monitoring. Their ability to serve as fluorescent markers enables high-resolution imaging in both in vitro and in vivo studies, making them ideal for tracking drug delivery processes or monitoring disease progression [10–12].

Moreover, integrating CQDs into hydrogel matrices further expands their biomedical applications, particularly in wound care and tissue engineering. Hydrogels, characterized by their high water content and their ability to create a moist environment conducive to healing, are widely used to promote cell migration, proliferation, and angiogenesis [13–15]. By incorporating CQDs, these hydrogels gain enhanced drug delivery capabilities and antibacterial properties while maintaining biocompatibility [16,17]. The fluorescent properties of CQDs can also facilitate non-invasive monitoring of wound healing. CQD-hydrogel composites hold particular promise for controlled drug release, where the hydrogel matrix gradually releases therapeutic agents in response to environmental cues such as wound exudate, providing localized treatment to improve healing outcomes.

The novelty of this study consists of the physicochemical characterization of CQDs synthesized using Watermelon seeds as a carbon source through hydrothermal treatment. Watermelon seeds are a green resource that is considered waste in Ecuador. Additionally, CQDs are incorporated into hydrogel matrices and the evaluation of their cytotoxicity and antimicrobial properties. Hydrgel-CQDs composites derived from this sustainable approach exhibit enhanced antimicrobial efficacy and minimal cytotoxicity, making them very promising for biomedical applications, particularly in developing advanced wound dressings. Therefore, this research which includes comprehensive structural analyses, hydrogel formulation, and biological evaluations, aims to demonstrate the potential of greenly synthesized CQDs as a sustainable and promising solution for developing innovative medical technologies.

The Table 1 compares CQDs synthesized via hydrothermal methods using different natural precursors, focusing on particle size and biocompatibility. Rice waste CQDs are the smallest (
2 . 70
nm
), while watermelon seed CQDs are the largest (
12 – 13
nm
), likely due to agglomeration. Most precursors yield low-to-slightly toxic CQDs, although data for Radiata pine and lemon waste is unavailable. Despite their larger size, watermelon seed CQDs remain biocompatible, making them a sustainable alternative to synthetic CQDs, which often contain toxic heavy metals. Their green synthesis supports eco-friendly applications in biomedicine and technology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots via hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CQDs using the hydrothermal method offers a sustainable and efficient approach to producing nanomaterials from biomass sources [22]. In this study, watermelon seeds were utilized as a renewable carbon precursor. This section outlines the sample preparation process, followed by a detailed procedure for CQD synthesis via hydrothermal treatment.

Watermelon seeds were categorized into three maturity stages: Level I (early green maturity), Level II (intermediate maturity), and Level III (fully ripe). After classification, the seeds were thoroughly washed with running water to remove surface impurities. Next, they were air-dried under natural sunlight for five days, ensuring complete moisture removal. Once fully dried, the seeds were ground into a fine powder using a mixer mill set at 25 Hz for 6 h. This material, referred to as ”the samples”, was subsequently homogenized at 12,700 rpm for 30 min to ensure uniformity. After homogenization, 2 g of the sample was dissolved in 50 ml of distilled water, maintaining a 2 g : 50 ml ratio. This liquid phase was first concentrated from 50 ml to 20 ml using a heating plate before hydrothermal treatment. This reduction was carried out in a bain-marie, using a heating plate, carefully maintaining a temperature range between 80 °C and 90 °C to prevent overheating. At the end of this process, the samples were left to room temperature and converted into a powder, which served as the precursor for the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs.

For the hydrothermal synthesis, we maintained a mass-volume ratio of 1:10, with 1 g of the powdered sample dissolved in 10 ml of distilled water. Immediately, the mixture was introduced into the Teflon (PTFE) chamber of a 100 ml stainless steel hydrothermal reactor and heated to 200 °C for 10 h. This controlled environment facilitated the carbonization of biomass and the formation of CQDs. After cooling the reactor to room temperature, it was observed that the resulting solution presented three distinct layers: a surface suspension, an intermediate layer containing dispersed CQDs, and solid residues that were deposited at the bottom of the reactor, as can be seen in Fig. 1. The liquid phase was carefully extracted and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to isolate CQDs. This process allowed the separation of the CQDs from other impurities, leaving the CQDs retained in the supernatant and impurities at the bottom. The complete hydrothermal synthesis process is illustrated in Fig. 2, demonstrating the transformation of watermelon seed biomass into functional CQDs.

Two different approaches were employed for this hydrothermal synthesis: (1) direct pulverized seed treatment, where the watermelon seeds were placed into the reactor immediately after pulverization without any additional processing, and (2) pre-treated samples as detailed in Section 2.1.1, where the seeds underwent a series of preparatory steps, including pulverization and homogenization, before hydrothermal treatment.

In both methods, the hydrothermal process consistently produced a three-layered separation: a top suspension, a CQD-rich liquid phase, and a sediment residue layer. The complete hydrothermal synthesis pathway, illustrated in Fig. 2, demonstrates the transformation of watermelon seed biomass into functional CQDs, underscoring the potential of this renewable, eco-friendly precursor for sustainable nanomaterial production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogel based on polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) fully hydrolyzed
ce:section.ce:para: Control Sample Preparation: A 5% PVA hydrogel was prepared by dissolving 0.6 g of fully hydrolyzed PVA in 12 ml of distilled water. The solution was stirred continuously at 40 rpm and heated to 140 °C for 2 h to ensure complete dissolution and the formation of a homogeneous, transparent gel.

Modification of PVA with CQD (PVA-CQDhydrgel): To incorporate CQD into the hydrogel matrix, the distilled water in the PVA control sample preparation was replaced with CQD solution. The PVA concentration remained at 5% and the CQD concentration was 0.03475 g/ml. This modification resulted in the formation of PVA-CQD hydrogel. The CQDs were uniformly distributed within the PVA matrix, forming a composite hydrogel.

Control Sample Preparation: To obtain an 8% HPMC hydrogel, 1.05 g of high-quality HPMC was dissolved in 12 ml of distilled water. Subsequently, the solution was stirred at 40 rpm at room temperature for 2 h, producing a uniform hydrogel.

Modification HPMC with CQDs (HPMC-CQD hydrogel): Similar to the PVA modification, the distilled water used in the control HPMC preparation was replaced with a CQD solution synthesized via hydrothermal treatment. The resulting HPMC-CQDs hydrogel had a polymer concentration of 8% and a CQD concentration of 0.03475 g/ml. Both the PVA-CQDs and HPMC-CQDs hydrogels were prepared using a bain-marie setup on a heating plate, ensuring controlled temperature conditions for optimal gel formation, as shown in Fig. 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial sensitivity test: agar disk diffusion method
ce:section.ce:para: The antimicrobial activity of the synthesized fluorescent CQDs was tested against Staphylococcus aureus ECBI-UITEY and Escherichia coli ATCC 25922 using the agar disk-diffusion method. The assay aimed to evaluate the antibacterial efficacy of CQDs and CQD-based hydrogels.

All materials were sterilized at 120 °C for 1 h before use. Then, we prepared Petri dishes with 10 ml of Mueller-Hinton agar, onto which
20
μ l
bacterial suspensions of Escherichia coli ATCC 25922 and Staphylococcus aureus ECBI-UITEY were inoculated. A McFarland 0.5 standard was used to ensure consistent bacterial density across all samples. Sterile disks were then impregnated with
10
μ l
of control antibiotic, CQD solutions, and CQD-based hydrogels (PVA/CQDs, HPMC/CQDs). The disks were carefully placed on the agar plates inoculated with Staphylococcus aureus ECBI-UITEY and Escherichia coli ATCC25922 and incubated at 37 °C for 18–20 h. After incubation, antibacterial activity was quantified by measuring the zones of inhibition around each disk. The results provided information on the antimicrobial efficacy of both CQDs and modified hydrogels.

We evaluated the cytotoxicity of the CQDs and CQD-incorporated hydrogels (PVA-CQDs and HPMC-CQDs) using the MTT colorimetric assay using the Roche protocol for cell proliferation (Roche, 2020). This assay assesses the metabolic activity of cells, providing a reliable measure of cell viability and proliferation.

To evaluate the cytotoxicity of CQDs and PVA-CQD and HPMC-CQD hydrogels, the ROCHE standard protocol for colorimetric cell proliferation assays was followed (Roche, 2020). The Roche Cell Proliferation Kit I, commonly known as the MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay, which is a widely used colorimetric method to assess cell viability and proliferation. cell in cell culture.

Firstly, we harvested the 3T3 fibroblast cell line using a cell scraper. This instrument allows cells to be scraped and collected easily and efficiently. Afterward, cell viability was evaluated with trypan blue. The medium used was Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) supplemented with 1% antibiotics and 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) as growth medium prepared in 1x and 4x concentrations.

Second, we prepared 96-well ELISA plates by adding cell culture medium to each well—Seed 3T3 cells at a concentration of 5×
1
0
4
cells/well. Specifically, we added 50 ul of 1x medium, 50 ul of 4x medium, and 50 ul of each compound at varying concentrations to each well, followed by 50 ul of cells. Positive controls comprised cells with media, while negative control comprised media without cells. Next, the prepared plate was incubated for 48 h at 37 °C in a humidified chamber. To achieve a color change 10 ul of MTT labeling was added to each well, followed by incubation for 4 h at 37 °C in a humidified chamber. After incubation, 80 ul of solubilization buffer was added to each well to dissolve formazan crystals. Finally, the absorbance was measured at 630 nm using a 96-well ELISA plate reader.

UV–Vis spectroscopy demonstrated the distinctive optical properties of CQDs synthesized from watermelon seeds. In the liquid phase, prominent absorption peaks were observed around 280–282 nm at the three maturity levels, as shown in Fig. 4, corresponding to
n →
π
∗
transitions of CO groups [23–26]. These findings align with the FTIR spectra, which revealed characteristic bands for unsaturated carbon–carbon bonds and carboxylic groups. A comparison between directly powdered samples and those homogenized and powdered showed that the latter method enhanced the optical properties, as evidenced by the higher absorbance. The optical results of maturity levels I, II, and III were more pronounced in the liquid phase after homogenization and pulverization. However, due to its statistical significance, the study focused on a detailed characterization of maturity level II (homogenized and powdered). A paired t-test comparing the UV–Vis spectra of Levels I, II, and III, with a significance threshold of
p < 0 . 05
, revealed that Level I differed significantly from Levels II and III. Therefore, Level II was selected for further analysis.

To accurately evaluate the as-synthesized CQDs, we employed high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) to characterize individual nanoparticles (Fig. 5). HRTEM images reveal that CQDs exhibit a quasi-spherical morphology with an average diameter of approximately 12–13 nm. A detailed lattice analysis shows a fringe spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of graphene, confirming their graphitic structure [27,28]. Complementary AFM measurements yield an average diameter of 13.4 nm. In addition, different CQDs were identified and analyzed in the AFM images, allowing a more detailed assessment of their morphology. Line profile analysis (Fig. 5c) highlights the topographic variations of individual CQDs, revealing an average height of 0.385 nm (Fig. 5d). Together, these high-resolution techniques consistently indicate that individual CQDs are within the 12–13.5 nm range, maintain a uniform spherical shape, and underscore that our synthesis method produces uniformly crystalline and quasi-spherical CQDs.

In contrast, the aggregation behavior of the CQDs was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), additional AFM imaging, dynamic light scattering (DLS), and Zeta potential measurements (Fig. 6). The SEM images reveal that the CQDs tend to form aggregates with quasi-spherical morphologies, where the aggregate sizes range between 140 and 141 nm [29,30]. Further AFM imaging of these aggregates confirms the formation of larger clusters. DLS analysis shows a broad size distribution, which can be attributed to the combined effects of aggregation and the presence of hydration layers in the colloidal solution [31]. Furthermore, zeta potential measurements indicate a relatively low surface charge of +2.36 mV, suggesting that weak electrostatic repulsion contributes to the formation of aggregates.

The Raman spectrum of CQDs shows characteristic G and D bands at approximately 1580
cm
− 1
and 1350
cm
− 1
, respectively [32], as shown in Fig. 7(a). The G band corresponds to hybridized carbon vibrations
s
p
2
, indicative of graphene or graphite-like structures within the CQDs [33,34]. In contrast, the D band arises from structural defects or edge vibrations in the graphene layers [35,36]. The
I
( D )
/ I
( G )
ratio of 0.85 suggests a moderate presence of defects, which could enhance the catalytic properties of the CQDs and modify their electronic behavior, making them suitable for various applications.

The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 7(b)) shows broad absorption bands around 3420
cm
− 1
, corresponding to O–H stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups. The peaks at 2920
cm
− 1
and 2850
cm
− 1
are attributed to the stretching of the aliphatic CH. Additionally, a strong band at 1720
cm
− 1
is associated with CO stretching vibrations of carbonyl groups, and the band at 1620
cm
− 1
corresponds to CC stretching in aromatic rings [25,26,32,37]. These functional groups suggest an oxygen-rich surface, enhancing the CQDs’ solubility and potential functionality in biological applications [38,39].

XPS analysis further supports the presence of oxygenated functional groups, as shown in Fig. 7(c). The survey spectrum confirms the presence of peaks C(1s), O(1s), and N(1s), indicating only the presence of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen elements [24,32,40].

Confocal microscopy images to observe fluorescence were captured using a Leica CMS GmbH confocal microscope. Highly luminescent CQDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method with watermelon seeds as the carbon source. The prepared CQDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV illumination at 365 nm. In daylight, the CQD solution appeared as a yellow transparent liquid. The yellow liquid color seen after hydrothermal treatment is documented in the literature [41]. However, exposure to UV light resulted in the emission of light at 365 nm, as shown in Fig. 8. Our preparation method provides a facile approach for large-scale production of highly fluorescent CQDs.

Furthermore, CQDs possess fluorescence that is distinguishable from water in the surrounding medium. This outstanding photoluminescent property can be utilized for imaging applications in vitro, as shown in Fig. 9. This strong fluorescence of CQDs in the blue range enables biomedical applications such as cancer [42]. However, it is key to consider the interference of undesired cell autofluorescence.
Moreover, the fluorescence of the CQDs is maintained when combined with PVA and HPMC, as evidenced in Fig. 10.

When CQDs mix with PVA and HPMC, several interesting properties related to fluorescence can be observed. For example, this combination can enhance the light-emitting properties of PVA and HPMC, resulting in more intense fluorescence for these hydrogels and stable fluorescence for the CQDs because PVA and HPMC can act as stabilizing agents for the CQDs, preventing aggregation and improving their dispersion. Furthermore, most fluorescent hydrogels containing CQDs are easy to fabricate and can be stored for long periods without losing their quantum yield (fluorescence) [43].

This combination can potentially achieve controlled drug release due to their strong fluorescence. PVA and HPMC can serve as effective drug-releasing matrices when combined with CQDs, enabling controlled and targeted drug delivery, taking advantage of the fluorescence properties of CQDs to monitor and visualize the drug release process in real time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial assays of CQDS, CQDs-PVA, and CQDs-HPMC
ce:section.ce:para: This study investigated the antimicrobial activity of CQDs, CQDs-PVA, and CQDs-HPMC. The findings suggest that none of them displayed antimicrobial effectiveness against E. coli ATCC 25922 and S.aureus ECBIUITEY at 1 mg/ml, 2 mg/ml, 4 mg/ml, or 30 mg/ml concentration. Table 2 presents a summary of these results. For the S. aureus ECBIUITEY strain, CQDS, and CQDs-HPMC showed antimicrobial activity at a concentration of 2 mg/ml, while no inhibition was observed for the E. coli ATCC 25922 strain.

The cytotoxicity of CQDs, PVA-CQDs, and HPMC-CQDs was evaluated in the Fibroblasts 3T3 cell line using the MTT colorimetric assay from ROCHE. This test quantified the correlation between cell viability and formazan production, with absorbance directly proportional to the number of living cells. The cytotoxicity of the compounds at concentrations of 0.03, 0.3, and 0.5 mg/ml was tested using an ELISA plate reader. Measurements were taken at 630 nm at 0 h, and again before and after 4 h. The results showed that the optimal time for the measurement was 4 h after starting since the saturation of the optical density (O.D.) levels, as shown in Fig. 11. On the contrary, measuring 4 h before was insufficient for the reaction to develop completely.

For future biological or biomedical applications, especially in clinical settings, CQDs must be non-toxic or exhibit low cytotoxicity. In this study, we evaluated the biocompatibility of CQDs and their combinations with PVA and HPMC. Cytotoxicity was assessed before and after 4 h of exposure to cells of the 3T3 fibroblast cell line. Our results showed that CQD alone, without any additives, demonstrated a high cell viability of 73.32%, suggesting that these materials are biocompatible and do not exhibit significant cytotoxic effects compared to the control group (100%) when the concentration is 0.03 mg/ml. The data regarding the compound concentration of 0.03 mg/ml follows a parametric distribution, leading to the execution of a one-way ANOVA. The results indicate no significant variances between the compounds examined and the control group.

However, the addition of PVA and HPMC to the CQDs resulted in a notable decrease in cell viability. In the presence of PVA, cell viability dropped to 31.50%, while with HPMC it decreased to 59.88%. These findings suggest that both hydrogels may induce cytotoxic effects when combined with CQDs, raising concerns about their safety for biological applications. This toxicity may be due to the interaction between the hydrogels and the cells, which could alter the cell properties and cause adverse effects on the cell membrane or metabolic activity.

Statistical analysis, performed using one-way ANOVA, did not show significant differences between CQD alone and the control group, confirming its biocompatibility under experimental conditions. However, this analysis also highlighted the need for further research, as the cytotoxicity observed in the CQDs combined with PVA and HPMC was not fully ruled out. Additional studies should explore lower concentrations of CQDs (below 0.03 mg/ml) and longer exposure times to more thoroughly evaluate the potential cytotoxic effects and interactions between CQDs and the hydrogels.

The synthesized CQDs from watermelon seeds suspended in water demonstrate biocompatibility, making it a promising option for future biological applications. Yet, the addition of PVA and HPMC raises concerns about their potential cytotoxicity. Further investigation is needed to optimize CQD concentrations and combinations and better understand the mechanisms underlying the observed cytotoxic effects.

This study successfully demonstrated the green synthesis of CQDs using watermelon seeds via a hydrothermal method, providing a sustainable approach to nanomaterial production. The comprehensive characterization revealed that the CQDs possess excellent optical properties, with a size of approximately 12–13 nm confirmed by HRTEM and AFM. They have blue fluorescence, as demonstrated by UV–Vis spectroscopy. XPS and FTIR analysis identified oxygen-rich functional groups, enhancing the solubility and biocompatibility of CQDs. Raman spectroscopy indicated moderate defect density, which may enhance their catalytic potential. The CQDs demonstrated slight antimicrobial activity against Staphylococcus aureus, both alone and in combination with HPMC. Cytotoxicity tests confirmed low toxicity at concentrations of up to 0.03 mg/ml, particularly when incorporated into HPMC hydrogels, maintaining slight biocompatibility. However, PVA at 5% concentration exhibited cytotoxicity, suggesting that lower concentrations should be considered. These findings imply the green-synthesized CQDs for biocompatible applications, with HPMC emerging as a preferred matrix for biomedical integration. Future research will optimize the hydrothermal synthesis parameters to improve antimicrobial efficacy and further functionalize CQDs to enhance their stability, solubility, and drug delivery capabilities. HPMC-based CQD hydrogels show promise for controlled drug release and noninvasive monitoring in wound care applications.

Marlene Puchaicela: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. David Lara: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Vinicio J. Cevallos: Methodology. Alexis Garzón: Methodology. Jules Gardener: Resources, Software. Guillermo Solorzano: Resources, Software. Lilian Spencer: Methodology, Formal analysis. Johnny Chimborazo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
